author: @h.waynemcconnell1364
text:
The Vortex Stove explored in Video # 1730 is perfect for supporting flame in a confined space and greatly reducing impingement. And the 3 curved plates circling the flame is the very best example to archive this that I have seen.
And combined with the second jacket as illustrated in Video 1741 provides a location for a copper water coil to be in the flue stream without being introduced to direct flame. This would work well just like the outdoor furnace so common in Canada.  The water would be circulated into the home and through a water coil in existing ductwork for example.
Now what I haven't seen yet is the introduction of a proper WASTE OIL burner at the base complete with an auto float system that maintains a constant level in the pot and does not use the more common fuel drip system which is subject to temperature, supply tank level, and potential clogging.
Your thoughts?

author: @DanHBExplained
text:
I was looking at these for a while, the BEST that I saw was to have a wide circular bottom for the air to come in from 2 holes and then a tall (see through) chimney.
The air rises but on entering the wide circular base below the fire through 2 holes, a wider circle concentrates more angular momentum as it approaches the bottom of the chimney to rise.
Maximizing rotation and so height.

Pyrolisis also creates extra hydrogen and oxygen due to flame spending extra time spiraling up the structure.

author: @C-M-E
text:
You may not have picked up on it Robert, but this is certainly your conscience telling you to build that jet engine. 😁
In the event you ever want to do this the slightly more direct way, you can even reuse your alky fuel can burner and simply add a swirl induction ring at the base of your primary induction holes. Intake air currents mixing with rising heat will do the rest. You can also help it along by directing your intake holes with cut lengths of short pipes angled up and towards the walls to induce the swirl, though a ceramic/clay hand-shaped ring is very easy to work with and will take high heat all day with a decent carbon coating. Basically just make a clay patty, push a tube from the outside up diagonally through each intake hole and push all the way through. Tada! 
Enjoy the mild roar when you really get it going! 
Add: Almost forgot, but for the sake of completeness, you can also drive this a little overboard by necking down a section of your chimney and expanding the remaining upper section into a venturi. The spacing from swirl ring to constriction point is heavily calculation dependent on burner size and internal volume, so unless you're going for all out optimization, it's definitely a lot more hassle than it's worth, but it Will most certainly get the housing cherry red hot!

There is an idea of power generator powered by a heated vortex like this. It's called atmospheric vortex engine (AVE).

One of the ideas is to use waste heat from nuclear plant to power the vortex so it would basically be a method of salvaging part of the waste energy and bring up the total efficiency of the nuclear plant.

author: @omnipresent2056
text: I wonder how much this vortex increases fuel burn. I know that in most heat exchanges (fires), pressure helps keep energy moving. Like in an ancient forge where they would use massive pumps to regulate the air flowing into the furnace. This is reverse pressure though, well reverse pressure that is increasing the intake of surrounding air which helps wick the fuel faster into the air creating a longer/larger airspace for the perfect fuel/air ratio. What is really fascinating is how it works perfect with 3 blades at a certain distance. This means that there's going to be a geometric "golden zone" for 3 blades, 4 blades, 5 blades, 6 blades and so on. I imagine that with the increase in blades it would lead to a higher internal spin because as you decrease the gap between blades the air flowing through the foils will increase in speed due to a higher pressure difference near the base of the flame vs the top of the flame. I also imagine that as you increase the spin speed (or the speed of the air entering the vacuum) this will either increase the potential height of the flame, extinguish the flame, or increase the total width of the flame (keeping the vortex low and flame closer to the walls) what would be very interesting to try is finding if there's a way to make two sets of blades. one set with a higher number of blades and a shorter air gap distance that sits either above or below a second set of blades with less blades and a larger air gap this would show that you can vary the amount of heat needed out of a system like this depending on the size of blade, number of blades, and gap between the blades.

replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Russian video shows rocket stove using heavy guage pipe located in centre of chimney pipe
Approx 20 small holes drilled into it
Holes are spaced evely throughout
This is for the secondary air addition to give it extra oxygen
Far as I can remember he didn't like that set up.... A later stove did away with the pipe and used 2 columns box-like drilled with holes that worked better
They were welded opposite to each other on the inside of the chimney pipe
I'm thinking that oxygen will complete the burning of any uncombusted material
Just an idea

author: @shanealexander9952
text:
Does it last the same amount of time?  and Off the Wall, Does it spin better one direction than the other?
The reasons I ask, 
1:  Toilets and Hurricanes spin naturally in different directions above and below the equator.  
2:  Natural phenomena like that seem to have 5 (fingers?) of intake in the vortex which are visable.  Some hurricane pics from satellite and some vortex on other planets that I've seen. Interesting with 3 intakes you got 2 strong vortex like dna.  Could that be 1, 2, 3, 5
3:  I am a little concerned about rapid increasing temperature in the fuel container.  Could turn the liquid into gas really quickly.
Might consider welding pins on one side of the pipe which go down into a base and a top, so they don't fall and the gaps can be adjusted.  
Like I have said a few times, I don't have space to do these things.  To me it is always a mental exercise.  I really enjoy your show, it keeps me thinking.

author: @vidard9863

i suspect that the two critical elements is that the "blades" must LIMIT air flow, and the chamber must be sized appropriately for the fuel. knowing how much air the blower would move i suspect that you would need a large fuel source and even then you would probably need to limit the sections that allow air through.
tldr: i suspect that if you used a perforated collar to let air in for secondary combustion it would be made more efficient by directing the air in such a fashion.

replies.author: @brandonlaird6876
replies.text: I definitely wish Rob had been my science teacher back in school also. Unfortunately, all my science teachers were interested in was mathematics. Don't get me wrong, I loved the mathematics as well, but they were *ONLY* interested in confirming that the mathematics were correct through testing; whether it was physics, chemistry, or genetics (a glorified AND chart for binary genetic traits). Rob, instead, introduces us to CONCEPTS and IDEAS, inflating our creativity, and I find that fascinating. (BTW, American here, and I deliberately used "mathematics" as a bridge between "math" and "maths" just to include both sides of the pond 😉)

its just the venturi effect of a fluid traveling in a direction in a confined space will create a low pressure zone that the denser fluid near that zone gets drawn in to fill the low zone
-then we get in to the flame plasma being electromagnetic in nature & so assisting the vortex confinement, just something else to not just dismiss but rather use if applicable-

author: @tichrontus
text: That's a good idea. Having air guided in to create a more controlled form of flame is definitely way better than a chaotic flame caused by turbulent air flow. The resulting longer flame length probably burns cleaner as well since unburned fuel gets longer exposure. But the centrifugal force might throw the fuel towards the chimney walls. I would keep the chimney diameter as small as possible so the flames have a chance to lick the inner walls clean.

author: @stephenowens5375
text: I made a cracking simple stainless vortex rocket stove years ago, 5" diameter riser with a 4" box section horizontal feed tube. The trick was to offset the riser so it was flush one side and 1" overhang on the other. Vortex was awesome with next to no smoke and amazing heat. Can't take all the credit as it was developed from another UK YouTuber called Ppotty. Check him out, he does some awesome work.

author: @BGW-25
text: Have seen that in real life. In a forest fire in SoCal. It was crazy and mesmerizing and scary all at the same time! Grateful I was far enough away up high to see it and not close enough to experience it!

replies.author: @markfryer9880
replies.text:
Such Fire Devils have been captured on video during Australian bushfires as well.  Some of our worst bushfires can even create their own weather system. During the 2020 fires when the Australian East Coast was on fire from top to bottom, ash from the fires was recorded as landing on glaciers in New Zealand!
Mark from Melbourne Australia

author: @woodworks2123
text: This reminds me of experiments at school many moons ago, the fire tornado in the metal wire basket bin. A cup of fuel is set alight in the middle of the bin, the bin is sitting on a powered turntable an when spun created a 4 ft high fire tornado. Awesome stuff but nobody ever really looked beyond the cool trick and actually looked at the practical uses. I did once see someone create a thermophotovoltaic generator using this tornado, creating a 4-5ft high flame and wrapped around with solar cells, the heat wasn't wasted as it was then used to heat water.

author: @mpedrozax1
text: Does the vortex use more fuel?  Does the vortex burn hotter?  Its absolutely cool but I wonder what benefits the vortex may provide.

replies.author: @mr.upcycle9589
replies.text: The vortex will draw in more air and mix the smoke gasses and air more effectively. So it burns hotter and more efficiently. As to whether it burns more fuel per minute, I would say it probably does because of the upward draw. But due to the massive increase in efficiency, over all you would burn less fuel to get more BTUs. Compared to a normal flame. So a lot of benefits to be had.

author: @goat-shaw
text: hmm does it use the fuel up way more when vortex

author: @Buzzhumma
text: Similar situation I find with the oil burners but in respect to creating direction with air intake . If it points upwards the burn efficiency increases as it creates a flame that goes upwards which helps burn more of the gases otherwise you can end up with a flame that doesn't extend in height and misses the gases that escape out of the top as smoke . The vortex works well to bring those gases in tight just like a reverse effect of a centrifuge.

replies.author: @f.d.6667
replies.text: Used to work for a company that made these commercially. We used ethanol as a fuel so soot was never a problem but there is a theoretical effect that could lead to better combustion. 3 vanes are sufficient, a tangential feed-in of air is best.

author: @justtinkering6713

replies.author: @Jarvis30
replies.text: Not an expert but i have been doing a lot of research lately into making furnaces, burn barrels, and a cremation chamber for pets and what i have picked up is that the vortex and direction of the spin can inprove the efficiency of the burn.  Most people are concerned about the flame being smokeless and that is just a part of being efficient.

replies.author: @Buzzhumma
replies.text: The air pressure created allows more air in for more complete burn . Its like someone gentle blowing on a fire .
